name: ServiceNow, Inc.

profile.description: ServiceNow, Inc. provides the leading enterprise AI platform, orchestrating 80 billion+ enterprise workflows annually across IT, HR, customer service, security, and finance. The Now Platform connects systems, data, and processes with AI to eliminate friction in enterprise operations. ServiceNow serves over 8,100 customers including 85% of the Fortune 500, with Q1 2026 subscription revenue of $3.67 billion growing 22% YoY. Founded in 2004, headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): ServiceNow has fundamentally transformed from an IT Service Management ticketing system (2004-2015) to an enterprise workflow platform (2015-2020) to an enterprise AI platform (2020-present), each pivot expanding the total addressable market by 10x+ — revenue grew from $7.2 billion in 2022 to $13.3 billion in 2025, with Q1 2026 accelerating to 22% YoY growth [1]. This is a textbook successful multi-stage business model transformation. AI Deployment (2/2): Now Assist AI capabilities are shipping to customers with measurable results — AI-specific commitments forecasted at $1.5 billion for 2026, customers spending over $1 million in annual contract value through Now Assist grew 130% YoY, and the company has automated 90% of customer service use cases while growing revenue 22% with headcount-neutral growth [1][2]. Market Share (2/2): ServiceNow is the undisputed category leader in enterprise workflow orchestration with 85% Fortune 500 penetration, no close independent competitor, and a current remaining performance obligations base of $12.64 billion growing 22.5% YoY [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The Armis cybersecurity acquisition (expected H2 2026 close), Moveworks AI integration, and agentic AI capabilities are named products with timelines, though specific next-gen platform releases lack detailed revenue projections [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): ServiceNow invests approximately 20% of revenue in R&D, above the application software industry average, funding the AI platform transformation [1].

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): ServiceNow's purpose — 'making the world work better for everyone' — is specific, service-oriented, and validated by two decades of execution transforming enterprise workflows from manual processes to automated, AI-orchestrated systems [3]. CEO Bill McDermott articulates this cause consistently, framing AI as amplifying human potential rather than replacing workers. The purpose has guided strategic decisions from ITSM to enterprise AI platform. However, it has not yet faced a true existential test that would elevate it to a 4. Community Impact (1/2): ServiceNow.org provides platform access to nonprofits, the company achieved 100% renewable electricity and carbon neutrality, and committed to net zero by 2030 — measurable sustainability commitments though the philanthropic programs are modest relative to company scale [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): ServiceNow consistently ranks on Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For and Glassdoor Best Places to Work, with CEO approval ratings above 90% and employees describing 'progressive culture, super smart and talented grown ups, no massive politics' [2]. Insider Buying (2/2): CEO Bill McDermott purchased $3 million in NOW shares personally during the March 2026 software sector sell-off and extended his contract through 2030 — meaningful open-market buying at personal financial risk during a period of market uncertainty [2].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics — near-perfect across all dimensions. D/E of 20.7% is well below the 70% target with $5.2 billion in cash against $2.4 billion debt [1][4]. ROE of 16.1% exceeds the 15% target (2x weight). Revenue growth of 22.1% YoY and 22.4% 3-year CAGR both substantially exceed the 10% target (2x weight on CAGR) [1]. Forward P/E of 20.3x is effectively at the 20x target, reflecting the stock's correction from $875 to $102 (post-split adjusted). Profit margin of 12.6% is the weakest metric, scoring 0.63 against the 20% target — explained by heavy stock-based compensation and reinvestment into AI infrastructure. PEG of 1.0 is exactly at the 1.0 target (2x weight), reflecting well-balanced growth-to-valuation. Non-GAAP operating margin of 32% and $6.28 billion free cash flow demonstrate the true cash economics obscured by GAAP SBC treatment [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): ServiceNow's core thesis is that AI creates more demand for enterprise workflow orchestration, not less — CEO McDermott argues 'AI excels at identifying problems and providing advice but cannot execute solutions' and ServiceNow controls the execution layer [2]. The platform orchestrates 80 billion+ enterprise workflows annually across deeply embedded, mission-critical IT, HR, security, and customer service processes with massive switching costs (NRR >125% sustained). Enterprise workflow execution requires integration with hundreds of legacy systems, compliance frameworks, and audit trails that AI cannot bypass. JPMorgan explicitly flagged ServiceNow as an 'AI-resilient' software name. However, the longer-term risk that agentic AI systems could eventually replace rather than complement workflow orchestration platforms prevents an IMMUNE rating [2]. AI Opportunity (1/1): AI-specific commitments forecasted at $1.5 billion for 2026, Now Assist generating measurable customer adoption with 130% YoY growth in $1M+ AI ACV customers, and the company itself has achieved headcount-neutral 22% revenue growth by automating 90% of customer service use cases [1][2].

infinite_game.summary: Bill McDermott is a rare enterprise software CEO who operates with genuine long-term platform thinking. His 'Rule of 55+' profitability standard (revenue growth + FCF margin > 55%), contract extension to 2030, and $3 million personal share purchase during the March 2026 sell-off reflect conviction in the company's cause. ServiceNow's transformation from ITSM ticketing to enterprise AI platform demonstrates existential flexibility, though the company's competitive language occasionally tilts toward finite game 'winning' rhetoric.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): ServiceNow's cause — making the world of work work better for everyone by eliminating enterprise friction — is genuine, service-oriented, and specific [3]. It describes a future state (frictionless enterprise workflows) that does not fully exist yet and invites technology partners, customers, and employees to contribute. McDermott has consistently articulated this cause through multiple economic cycles. However, it has not been tested through a true existential crisis, and the company's rapid TAM expansion sometimes feels more financially motivated than cause-driven. Display score: 7.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (2/2): ServiceNow consistently ranks on Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For and Glassdoor Best Places to Work, with CEO approval ratings above 90% [2]. Employees describe a 'progressive culture' with 'super smart and talented grown ups, no massive politics for a big company.' The company's 'human-AI' narrative — framing AI as amplifying employees rather than replacing them — creates psychological safety. McDermott's public statement that 'AI won't take your job, but someone using AI will' empowers rather than threatens the workforce. Display score: 10.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): McDermott publicly acknowledges Salesforce, SAP, and Microsoft as worthy rivals whose platform integrations push ServiceNow to develop better connectors and expand capabilities [2]. The Moveworks and Armis acquisitions reflect competitive awareness — rivals' strengths revealing ServiceNow's portfolio gaps rather than attempts to 'crush' competitors. Display score: 10.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The transformation from ITSM ticketing (2004-2015) to enterprise platform (2015-2020) to enterprise AI platform (2020-present) represents meaningful strategic evolution, with each pivot expanding TAM significantly [1]. However, these transformations were additive — building new capabilities on top of the existing platform — rather than abandoning a core business line. ServiceNow never had to 'blow up' a profitable legacy business to pursue its cause; it expanded from a strong base. This is evolutionary flexibility, not existential flexibility in Sinek's transformative sense. Display score: 6.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): McDermott's $3 million personal share purchase during the March 2026 software sector sell-off — when the stock dropped on AI disruption fears — is a concrete example of leading with conviction at personal cost [2]. The Rule of 55+ profitability standard maintains capital discipline. However, ServiceNow's investments are funded from abundant free cash flow ($6.28B) rather than requiring sacrifice, and the company has not walked away from profitable but misaligned business lines. Display score: 6.

investment_thesis: ServiceNow is the enterprise AI platform that benefits from — rather than is threatened by — the AI boom. Q1 2026 subscription revenue of $3.67 billion grew 22% YoY (accelerating from 19.5% in Q4), current RPO reached $12.64 billion (up 22.5%), and non-GAAP operating margin expanded 100bps to 32% [1]. AI-specific commitments are forecasted at $1.5 billion for 2026, with Now Assist customers spending $1M+ growing 130% YoY. Full-year 2026 guidance was raised to $15.74-15.78 billion in subscription revenue (20.5-21% growth). The stock has corrected significantly from its prior highs, bringing the forward P/E to 20.3x and PEG to 1.0 — a rare moment of reasonable valuation for the fastest-growing large-cap enterprise software company [4].
thesis_continued: The primary risk is that ServiceNow's current 20.3x forward P/E looks reasonable only because trailing P/E is 60.6x — the market is pricing in substantial earnings acceleration that depends on AI-driven margin expansion materializing as stock-based compensation normalizes [1]. GAAP profit margin of 12.6% masks the $6.28 billion free cash flow reality but also reflects real SBC dilution to shareholders. CEO McDermott's warning that new college graduate unemployment could reach 30% due to AI automation is both a bull case for ServiceNow (enterprises buy AI workflow tools) and a reputational risk (profiting from displacement) [2]. If agentic AI matures faster than McDermott's 'execution layer' thesis anticipates, enterprise workflows could be replaced rather than orchestrated, though the deep embedding across 85% of Fortune 500 provides substantial switching cost protection.
key_catalysts: AI-specific commitments exceeding $1.5 billion in 2026 as Now Assist adoption accelerates across the 8,100+ customer base, validating the AI-is-additive thesis
Armis cybersecurity acquisition closing H2 2026, expanding platform TAM to security operations and adding incremental revenue contribution
Non-GAAP operating margin expanding toward 35%+ as AI-driven productivity enables headcount-neutral revenue growth, demonstrating operating leverage at scale
key_risks: Agentic AI systems maturing to replace enterprise workflow orchestration within 3-5 years, faster than McDermott's 'execution layer' thesis anticipates — a risk that triggered the January 2026 software sell-off

GAAP profit margin of 12.6% reflects heavy stock-based compensation that dilutes shareholders — if SBC does not normalize while revenue decelerates, the 20.3x forward multiple would expand significantly
Geopolitical exposure: 35%+ international revenue faces currency headwinds (19% constant currency growth vs. 22% reported) and potential trade policy disruptions to enterprise IT spending
sources: ServiceNow Q1 2026 Earnings Release — subscription revenue $3.67B, cRPO $12.64B, April 22, 2026
ServiceNow CEO Bill McDermott commentary on AI, leadership, and $3M share purchase — Fortune, CNBC, Fast Company, 2026
ServiceNow Corporate Website — mission, ESG commitments, and leadership, servicenow.com, accessed May 2026
Yahoo Finance — NOW Key Statistics and Financial Data, accessed May 2026
